title_en: Introduction to an-Nawawi's Sharh of Sahih Muslim
title_ar: مقدمة شرح النووي على صحيح مسلم
content_en:
Yahya ibn Sharaf an-Nawawi stands among the most productive and widely read scholars in the history of Sunni Islam. Born in 631 AH in Nawa, a village in the Hauran region of Syria from which he took his name, he died young — in 676 AH at the age of forty-five — yet left behind a body of work that became foundational across multiple disciplines. His commentary on Sahih Muslim, known formally as al-Minhaj fi Sharh Sahih Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj, is among the most important of these works and remains in print and active use to this day.

What is remarkable about the Sharh is that an-Nawawi produced it relatively early in his career, before he had composed many of the later works for which he became famous. The commentary reflects a scholar in command of his field yet still developing the distinctive voice that would characterize his mature writings. Despite this, the Sharh on Muslim achieved almost immediate recognition as the standard commentary on the collection, a status it has never lost.

Islam ibn al-Hajjaj al-Qushayri al-Naysaburi compiled his Sahih with a different organizational logic than al-Bukhari. While al-Bukhari arranged his collection primarily by legal topic, Muslim organized his hadiths thematically and paid special attention to assembling all relevant chains for a single report in one place. This approach has advantages for the student of hadith transmission but can be less intuitive for the student approaching the collection for its legal rulings. An-Nawawi's commentary navigates this structure clearly, helping readers understand why Muslim grouped certain hadiths together and what the organizational choices imply.

The introduction to the Sharh, known as the Muqaddimah, is itself a substantial piece of scholarship on the hadith sciences. An-Nawawi discusses the definitions of sound (sahih), good (hasan), and weak (da'if) hadiths; the nature of Muslim's specific criteria for inclusion in his collection; and the scholarly debates about whether Sahih al-Bukhari or Sahih Muslim ranks higher in overall reliability. On this last question, he presents the standard position of the Egyptian and Maghrebi scholars — that Muslim is in some ways more technically rigorous in his chain-presentation — while noting that al-Bukhari is generally considered superior in knowledge of the narrators.

An-Nawawi's student years in Damascus placed him in a rich intellectual environment. He studied under many of the leading scholars of Syria in the seventh Islamic century, and the breadth of his reading is evident in the Sharh, where he regularly cites works of Shafi'i jurisprudence, Arabic grammar, and classical scholarship on theological questions. His training was deeply grounded in the Shafi'i legal tradition, though he engaged other schools of thought with characteristic care.

The Sharh on Sahih Muslim became the model for how a hadith commentary should handle the relationship between the text itself and the disciplines — legal, theological, linguistic — that bear on its interpretation. Its influence on the way Muslim students encounter the second most important hadith collection in Sunni Islam is immense and continues undiminished.
